topic: OpenAI Codex Memory Feature Guide: Persistent Work Sessions for Developers

key_points: April 16, 2026 'Codex for (almost) everything' update ships 90+ plugins, multi-day agents, and cloud storage mounts
Plugins authenticate via standard OAuth flows; IT/security teams should audit scopes and data-residency posture per connector
Multi-day agents can schedule future work and wake up automatically across days or weeks
Enterprise rollout with admin controls, memory governance, and audit logs coming soon
Persistent memory retains context across sessions, user preferences, recurring workflows, tech stacks, project conventions
Scheduled automations let Codex run in the background — not just when computer is open
Cloud-based triggers enable Codex to start without manual user intervention
Treats Codex 'less like a one-off assistant and more like a teammate you configure and improve over time'
Symphony framework — OpenAI's internal Elixir-based agent orchestration system — published to GitHub March 2026
Symphony is the same framework that powers Codex subagents internally
Codex powered by GPT-5.4 with 1M token context window, native computer use, and tool search
Custom agents configurable via TOML files in ~/.codex/agents/ or .codex/agents/
Subagents patterns now de facto industry standard across all major coding AI tools
April 2026: 90+ new plugins released alongside computer use and in-app browser
Computer use capabilities for desktop automation
Key new plugins: Atlassian Rovo, CircleCI, CodeRabbit, GitLab Issues, Microsoft Suite, Neon by Databricks, Render

stats.fact: Codex usage doubled since GPT-5.2-Codex launch in mid-December 2025
More than 1 million developers used Codex in the past month (as of April 2026)
90+ enterprise plugins shipped at the April 16, 2026 Codex update launch
Codex subagents support up to 6 concurrent agents per session
Persistent memory retains context across sessions, user preferences, recurring workflows, tech stacks, project conventions
Codex pricing changed April 2, 2026 from per-message to API token usage-based; starts at $20/month on Plus plan
4+ million weekly active developers use Codex by April 2026 — up from 3 million just two weeks earlier

angles: Deep dive on Codex memory architecture: how persistent context works across sessions and multi-day workflows
Practical guide to configuring memory for long-running projects: what to store, compression strategies, retrieval patterns
Memory governance for enterprises: audit logs, privacy considerations, GDPR compliance, data residency controls
Comparison with Claude Code memory (.claude/memory.md) and GitHub Copilot's organizational memory systems
Best practices for memory hygiene: avoiding context bloat, scheduled context pruning, priority-based retention
Multi-agent memory sharing: how Codex subagents share context while maintaining isolation boundaries
Memory-backed plugins: how Codex plugins leverage persistent context for workflow automation
Future roadmap: Codex memory expansion to cross-platform context sharing and real-time synchronization
outline: Introduction: The Evolution of AI Coding Assistants from One-Off Tools to Persistent Teammates
Understanding Codex Memory: What Gets Remembered Across Sessions
Architecture Deep Dive: How Codex Implements Persistent Context
Configuring Memory for Your Workflow: Settings and Best Practices
Enterprise Memory Governance: Security, Compliance, and Audit Controls
Multi-Agent Memory Sharing: How Subagents Coordinate Context
Memory-Backed Plugins: Leveraging Persistent Context for Automation
Memory Comparison: Codex vs. Claude Code vs. GitHub Copilot
Practical Examples: Real-World Memory Use Cases for Developers
Memory Hygiene: Avoiding Bloat and Optimizing Performance
Scheduled Automations: Setting Up Background Agents
Future Developments: The Roadmap for Codex Memory
Conclusion: Building a Long-Term Relationship with Your AI Coding Assistant
